frontmatter.title: Exponentials of the form i_!A \Rightarrow i_!B

mainmatter.p: Let \mathscr {C} and \mathscr {D} be cartesian closed categories, and let \rho ^* \dashv \rho _*: \mathscr {C} \to \mathscr {D} be an adjunction. Define \mathscr {G} to be the Artin gluing of the right adjoint { \mathscr {C} } \xrightarrow {{ \rho _* }}{ \mathscr {D} }. Involving \mathscr {C}, we have an adjunction j^* \dashv j_*: \mathscr {C} \hookrightarrow \mathscr {G}; involving \mathscr {D}, we have an adjoint triple i_! \dashv i^* \dashv i_*: \mathscr {D} \hookrightarrow \mathscr {G}. We have canonical isomorphism i^* { \mathopen {} \left ( {i_!B}^{i_!A} \right ) \mathclose {}} \cong B^A.

block.headline: Proof (Sketch).
block.p: We describe the actions of all these functors on objects below:

We recall the standard computation of the glued exponential i_!A \Rightarrow {i_!B} as the pulled back family below, in which the lower left map is defined using the fact that \rho _* preserves binary products:
We may simplify the above using our explicit computations of all the adjoints, noting that i^*i_! = 1 and j^*i_! = \rho ^*:
It is possible to construct a likely inverse to the lower composite. In particular, we will show that the following natural isomorphism is given by postcomposition with h:
(I didn't have time to finish writing that proof. Maybe there is a better way that doesn’t involve this awful computation? I believe that Marcelo Fiore has independently proved it this afternoon.)

mainmatter.blockquote: Interests: Mathematical models of computation; denotational and operational semantics of programming languages and interactive systems; type systems and program logics for functional and concurrent computation; applications of category theory to computer science.
